questions.question: Explain why CUDA kernels cannot return values directly and describe the standard technique for transferring results from the GPU to the host.
keyPoints: CUDA kernels are declared __global__ void because thousands of threads execute in parallel with no single caller for a return value
Results are written to device memory (allocated with cudaMalloc) by kernel threads
The host uses cudaMemcpy with cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost direction flag to copy results from device to host memory
For array results each thread writes to its own index; for scalar results reduction or atomicAdd is needed

questions.question: Discuss the various techniques available in CUDA for returning computation results from GPU kernels to the host. Compare atomicAdd and parallel reduction for computing scalar results, and explain the role of unified memory and pinned memory in data transfer optimization.
keyPoints: Basic pattern: cudaMalloc device memory, kernel writes results, cudaMemcpy DeviceToHost copies back to host
atomicAdd provides thread-safe accumulation but serializes concurrent writes to the same address, causing severe bottleneck with thousands of threads
Parallel reduction uses shared memory with tree-based combining: log2(blockDim) steps, stride halving, __syncthreads() between steps; thread 0 writes block sum to global memory
Reduction is much faster than atomicAdd because shared memory is fast and only one global write per block vs thousands of serialized atomic operations
Host completes reduction by summing the block partial sums after cudaMemcpy DeviceToHost
Unified memory (cudaMallocManaged) creates a single pointer accessible from both CPU and GPU, eliminating explicit cudaMemcpy but requiring cudaDeviceSynchronize before host access
Pinned memory (cudaHostAlloc) locks pages in physical RAM enabling direct DMA transfer without staging buffer, improving transfer bandwidth
Error handling: cudaGetLastError after launch for configuration errors, cudaDeviceSynchronize for execution errors
